audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel / Shemuel Bet chapter 8, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Philistine subjugation, Moabite line-measure killing, Hadadezer/Zobah war, horse hamstringing, Damascus casualties and garrisons, tribute and sacred treasuries, Toi's diplomacy, Aram/Edom textual variant, imperial administration, justice claims, foreign guards, and David's sons as priests/chief officials.

verses.restored: After this David struck Philistines and subdued them. David took Metheg-ammah from hand of Philistines.
He struck Moab and measured them with a line, making them lie down on ground. He measured two lines to put to death and one full line to keep alive. Moab became servants to David, bringing tribute.
David struck Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah, as he went to restore his hand at River Euphrates.
David captured from him one thousand seven hundred horsemen and twenty thousand foot soldiers. David hamstrung all chariot horses but left enough from them for one hundred chariots.
Arameans of Damascus came to help Hadadezer king of Zobah, and David struck twenty-two thousand men among Arameans.
David placed garrisons in Aram of Damascus, and Arameans became servants to David, bringing tribute. YHWH delivered/saved David wherever he went.
David took gold shields that belonged to servants of Hadadezer and brought them to Jerusalem.
From Betah and Berothai, cities of Hadadezer, king David took very much bronze.
Toi king of Hamath heard that David had struck all army of Hadadezer.

Toi sent Joram his son to king David to ask concerning his peace and to bless him because he had fought against Hadadezer and struck him, for Hadadezer had been a man of wars against Toi. In his hand were vessels of silver, vessels of gold, and vessels of bronze.
King David dedicated them also to YHWH, with silver and gold that he had dedicated from all nations that he subdued:
from Aram, Moab, children of Ammon, Philistines, Amalek, and from spoil of Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.
David made a name when he returned from striking eighteen thousand Arameans/Edomites in valley of Salt.
He placed garrisons in Edom; throughout all Edom he placed garrisons, and all Edom became servants to David. YHWH delivered/saved David wherever he went.
David reigned over all Israel, and David was doing justice and righteousness for all his people.
Joab son of Zeruiah was over army; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was recorder.
Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests; Seraiah was scribe.
Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over Cherethites and Pelethites, and sons of David were priests/chief officials.

translation_notes: The chapter is an imperial summary organized around striking, subduing, tribute, garrisons, plunder, and administration. It should not be softened into neutral national growth.

David's treatment of Moab—making captives lie down, measuring lines, and killing roughly two portions while sparing one—is mass execution. It is an atrocity, not a clever battlefield image or model of measured justice.
Hamstringing captured horses inflicts deliberate animal injury to disable military use. Ancient strategy does not remove animal suffering or create a modern practice to imitate.

Large casualty numbers and repeated statements that YHWH saved David belong to royal war theology and possible propaganda. They may not authorize modern holy war, militarism, or claims that victory proves righteousness.
Garrisons, servitude, tribute, and removal of metals to Jerusalem are structures of occupation and extraction.
Toi's diplomatic gifts are given in context of a shared enemy and shifting regional power. Diplomacy under empire may mix relief, strategy, fear, and tribute.
Verse 13 has a major textual issue between Arameans and Edomites, and the number/tradition differs across Samuel, Psalms, and Chronicles. The uncertainty must remain explicit.

The claim that David did justice and righteousness for all his people stands beside mass killing and foreign subjugation. Internal justice claims must be tested against treatment of outsiders and occupied peoples.
Cherethites and Pelethites appear as elite royal forces with possible foreign associations. Foreign identity does not imply disloyalty.
The description of David's sons as kohanim may mean priests or chief officials and raises questions about royal control of sacred and administrative office.
safety_note: Maximum anti-imperial safeguards: Do not glorify the Moabite executions, horse mutilation, mass casualties, occupation, tribute, or plunder. Do not use 'YHWH gave victory' as a modern war mandate. Evaluate royal justice by the lives of conquered peoples, animals, laborers, and communities under garrison—not only citizens at the imperial center.
